title: Trump administration withdraws appeal, securing historic victory for libraries and IMLS
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">Trump administration withdraws appeal, securing historic victory for libraries and IMLS</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<h3 class="text-align-center"><em>ALA&rsquo;s separate court challenge continues&nbsp;</em></h3>
<div>
<p>Washington &ndash; On April 6, a federal court granted the Trump Administration&rsquo;s request to withdraw its appeal of a federal judge&rsquo;s earlier ruling that struck down the Administration&rsquo;s dismantling of the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS)&mdash;the only federal agency dedicated to providing funding for the nation&rsquo;s libraries.</p>
</div>
<div>
<p>The dismissal brings to an end a lawsuit filed by the Attorneys General of 21 states in April 2025. In a decision issued on November 21, 2025, the federal District Court for the District of Rhode Island nullified the</body></html>

title: White House FY27 budget proposal repeats threats to eliminate IMLS
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">White House FY27 budget proposal repeats threats to eliminate IMLS</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<h3 class="text-align-center"><em>Advocates point to historic congressional support for libraries&nbsp;</em></h3>
<p>Washington &ndash; The proposed Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 federal budget released today by the White House would cut funding for the only federal agency dedicated to supporting all types of libraries, the Institute of Museum and Library Services (IMLS); the school library program Innovative Approaches to Literacy (IAL); and&nbsp;several&nbsp;library-eligible programs. The American Library Association (ALA) released the following statement from ALA President Sam Helmick:&nbsp;</p>
<blockquote><p>"We are not surprised that the White House FY27 budget zeroes out funding for IMLS and IAL. The president&rsquo;s intentions for IMLS wer</body></html>

title: 'Dangerous' bill inviting government censorship passes House committee
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">&#039;Dangerous&#039; bill inviting government censorship passes House committee</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<p lang="EN-US">Washington - In response to the March 17 Committee on Education and Workforce hearing, American Library Association President Sam Helmick issued the following statement on <a href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/7661/text">H.R. 7661</a>, a bill that threatens funding promised by the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of 1965 from any school that develops, implements, facilitates, hosts or promotes any program or activity for, or provides or promotes literature or other materials to, children under the age of 18 that include &ldquo;sexually oriented material&rdquo;:</p>
<p class="Indent1" lang="EN-US">&ldquo;H.R. 7661 is a dangerous bill that steals the power to choose what kids read away from parents, lo</body></html>

title: After blocking cuts to FY 2026 funding, library advocates call for boost in federal funding for FY 2027
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">After blocking cuts to FY 2026 funding, library advocates call for boost in federal funding for FY 2027 </span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<div>
<p>WASHINGTON &ndash; As the House of Representatives began taking requests for federal Fiscal Year (FY) 2027 spending, the American Library Association (ALA) launched its annual <a href="https://www.ala.org/advocacy/federal-resources/fund-libraries">Fund Libraries</a> campaign, mobilizing library advocates to request their Congressmembers sign bipartisan &ldquo;Dear Appropriator&rdquo; letters in supporting full funding for two programs.</p>
<p>Led by Rep. James McGovern (D-MA-2) and Don Bacon (R-NE-2) in the House, the FY27 &ldquo;Dear Appropriator&rdquo; letters call on Congress to provide robust funding (authorized at $232 million) for the Library Services &amp; Technology Act (LSTA) and $50 million for t</body></html>
snippet: <span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">After blocking cuts to FY 2026 funding, library advocates call for boost in federal funding for FY 2027 </span>
creator: IWARE

title: ALA denounces federal book banning bill
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">ALA denounces federal book banning bill</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><p>WASHINGTON - In response to <a href="https://www.congress.gov/bill/119th-congress/house-bill/7661/text">H.R. 7661</a>, introduced on February 24, American Library Association President Sam Helmick issued the following statement:&nbsp;</p>
<p class="Indent1">"Parents, not politicians, should guide their children&rsquo;s reading. In our school, campus, and public libraries, materials are selected by trained literacy professionals who understand child development and community needs. Their work is grounded in one clear purpose: helping young people become lifelong readers.&nbsp;</p>
<p class="Indent1">"H.R. 7661 isn&rsquo;t fundamentally about protecting kids. It&rsquo;s about giving politicians broad authority to restrict whose stories are allowed on our shelves. That should concern anyo</body></html>

title: American Library Association announces 2026 Youth Media Award winners
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">American Library Association announces 2026 Youth Media Award winners</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><p>CHICAGO &mdash; The American Library Association (ALA) today announced the top books, digital media, video and audio books for children and young adults &ndash; including the Caldecott, Coretta Scott King, Newbery and Printz awards &ndash; at its Youth Media Awards Announcements in Chicago.</p>
<p><strong>A list of all the 2026 award winners and honor selections follows:</strong></p>
<p><strong>John Newbery Medal</strong></p>
<p>John Newbery Medal for the most outstanding contribution to children's literature: &ldquo;All the Blues in the Sky,&rdquo; written by Ren&eacute;e Watson and published by Bloomsbury Children&rsquo;s Books, an imprint of Bloomsbury Publishing.</p>
<p><strong>Newbery Honor Books</s</body></html>

title: ALA, Leading Education Organizations Highlight Value of Technology for Learning as Senate Committee Examines Kids’ Screen Time
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">ALA, Leading Education Organizations Highlight Value of Technology for Learning as Senate Committee Examines Kids’ Screen Time </span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<p class="text-align-center"><em>In joint letter, groups urge policymakers to distinguish education technology from recreational screen time; recognize need for appropriate guardrails to protect kids</em></p>
</div>
<div>
<div>
<p>Washington, D.C. (January 15, 2026) &mdash; Ahead of today&rsquo;s Senate Committee on Commerce, Science and Transportation hearing on youth screen time, 17 national education technology, library and nonprofit organizations sent a joint letter to Chairman Ted Cruz and Ranking Member Maria Cantwell highlighting the importance of defining screen time and recognizing the beneficial role of education technology and connectivity in K-12 learning.&nbsp;</p>
<p></body></html>

title: American Library Association Kicks Off 150th Anniversary with Youth Media Awards
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">American Library Association Kicks Off 150th Anniversary with Youth Media Awards</span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><p><strong>CHICAGO&nbsp;</strong>- The&nbsp;<a href="https://ala.org/">American Library Association</a> (ALA) officially launched its&nbsp;<a href="https://ala150.org/">150th anniversary year</a>, marking a century and a half of empowering library professionals, advancing access to knowledge and protecting the freedom to read. Founded in 1876, ALA has&nbsp;advocated for all libraries and library workers to ensure equitable access to information for all.</p>
<p>&ldquo;Libraries are lifelines, shared public spaces, and engines of opportunity,&rdquo; said ALA President Sam Helmick. &ldquo;This milestone year is a moment to inspire, to rally our members and to invite all library professionals and advocates into the shared mission of</body></html>

title: ALA welcomes pathbreaking study on the state of the nation’s library facilities
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">ALA welcomes pathbreaking study on the state of the nation’s library facilities </span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<h4 class="text-align-center"><em>Report finds thousands of public libraries with a building system in poor condition&nbsp;</em></h4>
<p>WASHINGTON &ndash; The American Library Association (ALA) today welcomed the <a href="https://www.gao.gov/products/gao-26-107262">release of a major national study</a> about the condition of public library buildings. The study was conducted by the U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO), a nonpartisan federal research agency, at the request of Congress. ALA advocated for the study to be undertaken.&nbsp;</p>
<p>The GAO report provides the first comprehensive national data in decades about the state of the nation&rsquo;s library facilities. Nationwide, there are more than 17,000 public libra</body></html>

title: Supreme Court refuses to hear Little v. Llano County: ALA responds
content:
<html><head></head><body><span class="field field--name-title field--type-string field--label-hidden">Supreme Court refuses to hear Little v. Llano County: ALA responds </span>

                            <div class="clearfix text-formatted field field--name-body field--type-text-with-summary field--label-hidden field__item"><!DOCTYPE html>
<html><body><div>
<p>WASHINGTON &mdash; <em>The American Library Association released the following statement in response to the December 8th decision by the Supreme Court of the United States to decline to review the Fifth Circuit Court of Appeals en banc decision in Little v. Llano County.</em></p>
<p>The American Library Association (ALA) expressed disappointment and concern following the U.S. Supreme Court&rsquo;s decision to allow a lower court ruling from the Fifth Circuit to stand. This decision will significantly restrict the freedom to read in public libraries across Texas, Louisiana, and Mississippi. ALA President Sam Helmick said,&nbsp;</p>
<p>"By declining to review the Fifth Circuit&rsquo;s decision, the Supreme Court has empowered state and local government</body></html>
